FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,066, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables separately: texting, video chat, video games, social networking, TV, videos. From abstract: 'contemporary screen time modalities (e.g., video streaming, video chatting, texting and social networking)'

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses linking baseline screen time (exposure) to BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up (non-SM outcome). Table 2 reports coefficients for each modality.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective cohort study with multiple linear regression analyses, baseline and 1-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, Pediatric Obesity, 2021, DOI: 10.1111/ijpo.12827

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (exposure), not the dependent variable. BMI percentile is the outcome (non-SM). EC7 does not apply.
notes: All FT-IC criteria clearly MET. Prospective design with specific smartphone/SM exposures and non-SM outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,875 participants, ages 9-10 years, nationwide sample

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary focus is on generic 'total screen time' composite aggregating multiple modalities (TV, videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat). Although paper examines specific modalities in Table 1, the primary exposure variable reported across most analyses is 'Total Screen Time,' an aggregate measure. Per FT-IC2 guidelines, mixed-modality papers with only aggregate composite reported = NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Correlation and regression analyses conducted. Table 2 shows multiple linear regressions with screen time as predictor and various outcomes (depression, anxiety, academic performance, etc.)

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports standardized betas and p-values (e.g., Main effect of ST on Depression: β=0.018, p=.214)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional and correlational analyses from ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, PLoS One, 2021, DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0256591

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable in analyses; mental health, academic, and social outcomes are dependent variables (non-SM outcomes). Cross-sectional design. EC7 does not apply.
notes: EXCLUDED per FT-IC2: Primary exposure is aggregate total screen time composite, not separately extractable smartphone/SM-specific coefficients. While paper reports some specific modality results in tables, the conceptual framing and primary analyses use undifferentiated 'Total Screen Time.'

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, subsample of 67 participants (ages 11-12 years), pilot substudy described as part of ABCD implementation

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures passive sensing of smartphone screen use by app category (streaming, communication, gaming, social media). From abstract: 'passively sense smartphone screen use by time and types of apps used.' Table 1 breaks down app categories.

FT_IC3.evidence: Paper reports correlations between passive sensing data and self-report (r=0.49) and parent report (r=0.61), but these are measurement concordance tests, not associations between smartphone/SM and external non-SM outcomes. No inferential tests linking smartphone use to behavioral, mental health, or clinical outcomes reported. Per E5, measurement papers must also report phone/SM↔non-SM association; this paper only validates measurement agreement.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports correlation coefficient r=0.49 (between passive sensing and self-report) and descriptive statistics (mean app use times in Table 1)

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a measurement/feasibility paper describing passive sensing protocol and validating EARS app concordance with self/parent report. No original analysis linking smartphone use to external outcomes. Per guideline: 'psychometric-only (factor structure / reliability without external outcome)' = NOT_MET

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, JMIR Mental Health, 2021, DOI: 10.2196/29426

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC5 (measurement-only without external outcome analysis); FT_EC7 directionality check is moot
notes: EXCLUDED per FT_IC5: Measurement/methodology paper with no external outcome analysis. Paper validates passive sensing method but reports no associations between smartphone use and behavioral, mental health, academic, or other non-SM outcomes.
